summary: O'Neil argues that student debt from predatory for-profit colleges should be forgiven as a 'sunk cost' that the government incurred through regulatory failure. She explains how for-profit colleges like Corinthian, ITT Tech, and University of Phoenix gamed the federal aid system through questionable accreditation and deceptive marketing to poor students. While the government hesitates due to expense and precedent concerns, O'Neil contends the debt will never be repaid anyway, so acknowledging it as a sunk cost allows for learning from the mistake and fixing the broken accreditation system.
key_points: For-profit colleges exploited federal student aid by obtaining dubious accreditation and lying to vulnerable students about educational outcomes
The DOE's failure to maintain accreditation standards created this debt crisis, making the government responsible for the losses
Framing unpayable debt as a 'sunk cost' rather than hoping for repayment enables proper problem-solving and systemic reform
The DOE has begun addressing the root cause by tightening accreditation standards and shutting down problematic accreditors
Debt forgiveness is the practical conclusion since the money will never be recovered from students who were defrauded
money_quotes: I claim these are sunk costs and should be treated as such.
Once you let your standards go that far, you are on the hook.
Although it looks 'expensive' to forgive this debt, there's really no other option, because it's never getting paid back.
That's what happens when you let a predatory industry prey on the most vulnerable.
What's good about acknowledging sunk costs is that you can learn your lesson and fix the problem that got you into this mess.
When you don't acknowledge sunk costs, you're in the wrong mindset, hoping against hope that somehow the money will be paid back. It won't.
We need to turn off the federal aid spigot for bad colleges.
key_insight: Treating for-profit college debt as a sunk cost rather than a recoverable asset allows the government to stop wishful thinking and actually fix the broken accreditation system that enabled predatory lending.
